speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4, interview with Judith Velencei. We were talking about the incident at the labor camp. Can you continue, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That interrogation ended with a little bit happier note after I got the message from this police who let me know that's not true. Of course, those days, I didn't know he was beaten. But at least I knew he's not dead. And we were taken to a jail. And we had to spend the night in that jail in [INAUDIBLE]. And that was one of the longest night I spend in my life. I wasn't sure if we're going to be killed. After this interrogation ended, it was some hint of that's it, and they're going to finish us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I believed it. Why not? By that time, I knew how much bad things going on. So when my mom and I got alone, back in a jail, I said to her, please don't let me fall to sleep. I want to stay up all night. I want to live as long as I can. I have a lot to think about, and I don't want to sleep through my last hours. So she promised me she will pinch me, she will do something, but she was not going to let me sleep. And I-- was a very cold night. I even remember the train of thought. I thought, I am only 12, never been in love, never been married, never had children, home, anything I didn't do. And I may have to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just realized I never even saw a sunset up. I had seen the sunset, many night, you see, or the sun down, I had seen. But the sunset was-- all this happened before I would have to get up. So I said to my mom, be sure you remind me when it gets a little light. And we had a tiny little window to the yard. Maybe we see the sun will come up. And I said, maybe that much more I will experience in my lifetime, which we did. I was up all night. And it was around 7:00 or 8:00 when they finally came to get us. I didn't know where we'd go. Nobody said anything. We walked through this courtyard. And every flowers, every weeds, I stared at. And I looked at-- maybe it's the last time. My mom didn't-- said nothing. Just hold my hand. But we were taken to the railroad station, not back to interrogation. So we knew we had a little lease of life again. And it was another interesting episode on that railroad station, because I accidentally bumped into a school friend from that boarding school who was the daughter of the head-- the master of the railroad station. I don't know what the title of this man. And that girl was in school in my same class in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And she recognized me. And I, of course, recognized her. And my mom had the guts to ask that brutal man and say, would you do me a favor and ask these two policemen to stay a couple steps behind so this little girl wouldn't know my daughter is being taken someplace? And they did. So we had a nice and happy chat. The girl never knew I am under guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The train came in. We all got on. And we were very happy to recognize familiar grounds. We were heading back to Miskoic. My mom even asked the man, where are we going? And till the last minute, he didn't said. But he said, I must take you to the police station because we need documentations. And those people will be able to-- more familiar with the local situation. And they will have some kind of evidence by now. So we were taken to the police station, booked in like criminals, and taken down to the jail. It was a formal, regular jail, where people were waiting for their trial and so on, and so on. But we were taken into a smaller room where were just another woman who was on murder charge, which turned out to be wasn't true. And we were staying with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had a chance to observe prison life. And every time I had to go to the bathroom, and the police walked me to the bathroom, I looked in every window. And the saddest thing I saw-- there was two huge rooms. There's a couple of them like ours. But there was two huge rooms in the end. And one of them was full of Jewish people. I guess they were hiding. All the Jewish people were already deported to Auschwitz. And they find this group. There was mostly women and children, but there were some older men between them. And they were also very curiously looking out and looked at me. And I waved to them. They waved back. But what sad faces. I never forget some of those children I saw through the window. Next day, they were taken. I never saw them again. But we did stay for almost two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked-- they looked Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had no other identification on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No identification. But they were Orthodox Jewish. Women had their head in this kerchief. The mens was bearded. A And they had the side curls. So did the children. They were very poorly dressed. They looked like ordinary country people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never had a chance to talk to them, just waved to each other. And I spent two weeks, very interesting two weeks. I always considered that as an education, because most of the people who turned around there were prostitutes, picked up during the night. [LAUGHING] And they had to put them into that station. And of course, my education got kind of broad under that two weeks, not matter how much my mom tried to protect me and steer my attention away. But a lot of things, she couldn't prevent. And I learned some dirty words. And I learned a couple of things in there which my mother wasn't very pleased. But overall, they were nice as humans. They were sneaking chocolates. And they gave it to me. And they really became friendly with, with me. They were very sorry to have a kid in a jail. And during the bombing raids, when we all was in one big place, and there was some dirty language going on, then there was always at least one of them who would say, shut up. There is a kid in the place. Don't talk like that. And they would shut up. So I really can say some of those women were very good persons. Matter of fact, one of them, I happen to bump into after the war. And I recognized her. And I was smiling and was ready to approach her, and with her on that public street, a lot of people walking, and she didn't want it, that encounter for my sake. And she quickly turned away and went under one of those walking doors. You know, I'd-- we call it [NON-ENGLISH]. It's between the house and backyard. It's a big, arched [? wave. ?] And she disappeared in one of them so I couldn't talk to her. But I was ready to declare my friendship after that. But anyway, it was fun time, too. We were very concerned of what's going to happen. But thanks to one of the policeman who was also sympathetic and asked us why we are here, and my mom asked her-- asked him to take a message to my uncle. And he did. And once my uncle find out we are in this prison, he started to move mountains to get us out. And he happened to be in a friendly term with the governor of the county. And he approached the governor. And it just happened to have a loophole in the Jewish laws during that period, which may not last, then, very long, but that was a loophole, a kind of easement, for half-breeds, like I was considered to. And he gave a paper to my uncle, which allowed my uncle to pick us up and take us out of the prison. So he came, and he rescued us. And we didn't have to report anyplace. He wanted us immediately to go to his house, but it wouldn't be safe any place on a long run.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mom decided as long as we have this paper which allow us to be free, she would prefer to go home and look around the house and change clothes, pick up fresh things, and get prepared for the next steps and see Grandmother and the aunts and everybody. So that's what we did. We went back home, but we kept a very low profile.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was constantly supervised by my family, my Catholic family. They came in and cleaned, aired it out, and so on, so on. So it wasn't anything unusual, family coming and going to this house. And we were inside, but we tried not to go out. The window shade was pulled down toward the street. Only toward the courtyard it was open. And we stayed home for about a week or two. I didn't go out during that time. My mother did, who was accustomed to be out and free. And a funny thing happened during that time. One of my aunt, who was the secretary to the mayor of that town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this in Miskoic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in Tibolddaroc, in the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I just stop you for a minute--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and just go back? Did you see the signature of the person who signed the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall that person's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. His name was Borbely-Maczky. And he was a very famous political figure. When I use the term governor, I may-- I don't emphasize enough how important figure he was. But he was a head of a big section of Hungary, which included our little municipalities, too. And I learned later on, he was killed. He was beaten to death by Jewish people before he was able to put into trial. But in this one instance, he happened to do a favor to us, thanks to my uncle, who was able to get it out of him one way or the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So because of his name on this paper, we were feeling safe enough to stay home for a while. But by that time, it was toward the very dangerous time of the Szalasi regime, which was the most brutal Nazi regime Hungary ever had. And he became the ruler of Hungary in September. And this is the time when we were happen to be staying in, in Tibolddaroc. So when we heard about that news, we knew we have only a couple more days, perhaps, to stay in peace. And that same time, there was a, a whole busload of organizers coming into town who were setting up this Szalasi party, which called Arrowhead Party. They were organizing it. And my aunt, who happened to work in the town hall, heard about this group coming out. She didn't even knew if it's going to be an armed group or just civilians or what.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she called my mom, and she said, I think you better get out of town as long, as soon as you can, because these people is coming, and we don't know what they are up to. But there was no way to get out. You couldn't trust anybody and ask where's a horse and wagon to take us to the railroad station? It's taking two, three hours with a horse and wagon. Anybody could pick us up on the way. It was no way to do it. So my mother, again, pulled her trick out of the hat. She told me to get dressed very well. And we put on our best, most elegant outfit we could. And she said, only take our pocketbook, and come with me. We walked to the town hall. The bus was still in front of the building. She approached the bus driver, who was in army uniform of the Arrowhead people, which was a black uniform. And he said to the chauffeur, would you mind to take two passengers into the city of Miskoic? And he said, oh, I think we have enough room, and we are heading to Miskoic. Oh, I'm sure my boss will approve. Let me talk to him. He got the approval. And by the gang came out of the building, we were already sitting in the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in, said hello to us, and we said hello to them. They had no idea who they are taking to Miskoic. They were very friendly. And I knew they liked my mom, who was a very attractive lady. And she kind of flirted with them. And we had a very pleasant drive into the city. They wanted to know where we heading. They would take us all the way to the house. But my mother said no, we're going to meet in a restaurant with some friends. So they dropped us off in the center of town. And they left, and then we quickly went up to my uncle's place. In the meantime, from the period of picking us up at the police station and the time we arrived with the bus, he was working nonstop to make us a place, which was a cave located in a cellar. It was a big wine cellar, which was officially a bombing shelter during the war. But he digged a hole under this bomb shelter, and luckily, he didn't have to work overly too much, because once he went down in a tunnel, he happened to fall into a natural cave. So that was a size almost like this room, which was a clear space. He only had to make that tunnel. He didn't know that he will end up with that big [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So immediately, he was able to bring down woodwork and created a wood bed, bring down warm pillows and everything he could. There was two-- well, he only left this tunnel between the shelter and this cave. And he rolled a barrow over this tunnel. So from the outside, nobody knew there it is a hole under the barrow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had no electricity, naturally, but I was given candles. And I was also told try not to use the candle too much, because it takes oxygen. And they was not sure how much oxygen is going to be used and how much is going to be needed or how long I'm going to be there. So they were concerned of that. So they said to try to keep it to the minimum. And there was just a bucket for the waste. And once a day, there would be another bucket coming down with food. And they would-- I would put my bucket on that hook, and they would take it away. And they would do that first thing in the morning before-- because usually, the bombing happened during the night and very rarely during the day. And it was a terrible experience. I was there for, for eternity. It looked the longest period of my life. I had nothing to do. I couldn't read. I had absolutely nothing. I was cold, damp, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the most scariest part was when, during the bombing, I heard the people above me crying and praying and be afraid. And I didn't have anybody to cry to or being comforted. Those people were comforted with each other, and I had to be alone. And I was afraid if something happened to the bomb shelter, maybe a bomb will hit them. Maybe my uncle will die, too, and nobody will ever know I am down here. Nobody knew beside him. So his life was as important as mine, because if he dies, I'm gone. And it was a very scary experience. Toward the end, the last two, three weeks-- but for six weeks I was definitely all by myself. But toward the very end, my father arrived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, who happened to be transferred from the [INAUDIBLE] ghetto with most of those selected people into a safer area, because [INAUDIBLE] was at the Slovakian border. And there was a lot of partisan activity there. Some of the Jewish people, including my stepfather, who was also-- later, stepfather-- who was also important at camp escaped and became a partisan. And that find very dangerous surroundings. So they decided to take them to the trans-Danube area, which was far more safer. And this people was taken into that new location. And if you ever read or heard about the face of that camp, which called the Pusztavam, everybody was murdered there. And to my greatest surprise, all the books who mention the Pusztavam, they just say all those saved doctors and lawyers and important intellectuals, they all perished after they were forced to dig their own grave. And nobody knew there was three survivors. And one of them happened to be my father. I even knew the other two guys. One was a dentist. The other was a doctor of internal medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to the doctor, but I remember the dentist, because he became my dentist after the war. He was Szasz, Karoly, a very nice, big, strong man. I do remember the doctor, too. He happened to be my doctor, too. But he had a very common Jewish name, and I am not sure if I-- [? Fleischman ?] or something like that. And they happened to be out of town of Pusztavam together with a guard. They went to the next town to pick up food for the camp. They were away from very early morning hours till afternoon, late in the afternoon. And when they were coming back, they noticed a big [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bump in the ground, which were not there. It was a freshly pile of ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father was keep watching it. What, what is that? And it was getting dark. And the air was getting cool. It was late, late in the fall season. And he noticed it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] steaming. There was steam coming out from that dirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't said nothing to his fellow travelers. But when they went into the camp, the whole camp was empty. And they go to the head of the camp. I remember, my father even mentioned his name. His name was Dezseri, and he was the captain. And then that guy saw these three Jews with the guard. He thought he's seeing ghosts.

speaker: interviewer
text: It took him time before he realized, my god, these three men just came back from their duty. And he signed a paper to the Nazis. He gave the whole camp into their hand. He had to count for every name. And he signed his name, that's everybody. And when he saw that three guy, he got so panicky, he couldn't think. He said, what am I going to do? And my father was the first one who came to his senses, and he said, don't worry. Just let us go. We're not going to tell anybody we are part of this group. Just let us go. So he said, go, go right away. And then my father said, would you lend us this guard, also? Because in this outfit, and with our look, we couldn't walk to the next town till people will know we have escaped. So if you give us this man and he can take us to a safer area, then we have a chance to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did. And this guard, that's a sergeant, who walked them into the area where there was that partisan activity. And they had every intention to join the partisans. And that happened to be a place close to Miskoic. And he drug them off in there, in the woods. And the three men decided, well, they, instead of looking for the partisans, they going to find themselves a safe place. And my father knew he only had to find somehow his way into Miskoic. There is a lot of people there who can take care of him. So he departed from the others, too. And that's how he came back to Miskoic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father told you this story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Soon after he joined us in a cave.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to stop the tape now. We will continue on the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
